name: Zahabi, Maryam
recent_1: Kashyap, L.D., Wang, Z., Chang, Y., Zahabi, M., & Garcia, A. (2026). Inferring Hidden Attentional States in Driving: A Bayesian Approach to Modeling Distraction and Secondary Task Engagement. Human Factors, 68(8), 1098-1123. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261430069
recent_1_synopsis: This paper develops a Bayesian computational framework to infer latent attentional states of drivers—specifically distraction and secondary task engagement—from observable behavioral signals. By modeling hidden cognitive states probabilistically, the approach offers a principled method for real-time driver monitoring without requiring explicit secondary task declarations. The work advances human performance modeling for intelligent transportation systems by enabling inference of unobservable mental states.
recent_2: Basnet, N., Allahvirdi, S., Nadri, C., Park, J., & Zahabi, M. (2025). Evaluating the Feasibility of EMG-Based Human–Machine Interfaces for Driving. Human Factors, 68(1), 123-141. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251367179
recent_2_synopsis: This study examines whether electromyography (EMG) signals can serve as a viable input modality for human–machine interfaces in driving contexts. Participants performed driving tasks while EMG data were recorded and classified to assess control accuracy, workload, and usability of the interface. The findings establish feasibility benchmarks for EMG-based HMI in automotive applications, with implications for both assistive technology and driver state monitoring.
recent_3: Basnet, N., & Zahabi, M. (2025). Real-time cognitive workload assessment using non-intrusive methods: a systematic review. Ergonomics, 1-26. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2025.2606779
recent_3_synopsis: This systematic review synthesizes findings from 50 peer-reviewed studies examining non-intrusive physiological and behavioral methods for real-time cognitive workload assessment, including ECG, PPG, EDA, EEG, eye-tracking, and skin temperature. The review identifies current methodological trends, technological advances, and gaps in the literature regarding unobtrusive workload monitoring in operational settings. It provides a consolidated evidence base for practitioners designing cognitive state-aware adaptive systems.
seminal_1: Zahabi, M., Kaber, D., & Swangnetr, M. (2015). Usability and Safety in Electronic Medical Records Interface Design: A Review of Recent Literature and Guideline Formulation. Human Factors, 57(5), 805-834. DOI: 10.1177/0018720815576827
seminal_1_synopsis: This highly cited review (~383 citations as of June 2026, per Google Scholar) examined usability and safety issues in electronic medical record (EMR) interface design, surveying human factors literature and safety incident reports. The authors synthesized design guidelines targeting diagnosis and documentation workflows, providing a foundational reference for HCI research in health information technology. Its breadth and practical guideline output have made it a standard citation in EMR usability and patient safety research.
seminal_2: Zahabi, M., & Abdul Razak, A.M. (2020). Adaptive virtual reality-based training: a systematic literature review and framework. Virtual Reality, 24(4), 725-752. DOI: 10.1007/s10055-020-00434-w
seminal_2_synopsis: This widely cited work (~321 citations as of June 2026, per Google Scholar) presents a systematic review and integrative framework for adaptive VR-based training, identifying key components: performance measures, adaptive logic, and adaptive variables across multiple domains. The framework has become a standard reference for researchers and practitioners designing VR training systems that respond dynamically to learner performance. Its cross-domain applicability—from aviation to rehabilitation—explains its rapid and sustained citation uptake.
expertise_overview: Maryam Zahabi's research centers on human systems integration, with particular emphasis on human performance modeling, cognitive workload assessment, and human–machine interface design in high-stakes operational environments. Her work addresses driver behavior and in-vehicle technology, including law enforcement mobile computer terminals, advanced driver-assistance systems, and EMG-based control interfaces for driving and prosthetic devices. She employs physiological measurement (EMG, EEG, ECG, eye-tracking), cognitive performance modeling, and adaptive systems design to evaluate usability and safety. A significant thread of her research examines police officer cognition and performance under dual-task and pursuit-driving conditions, combining applied field studies with computational modeling approaches. Additional research directions include adaptive virtual reality for training, automated vehicles for older adults with cognitive impairment, and assistive technology usability for persons with disabilities. Methodologically, she integrates human factors experimentation, systematic reviews, Bayesian and computational modeling, and user-centered design evaluation.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. Sources: Texas A&M ISEN faculty profile (https://engineering.tamu.edu/industrial/profiles/zahabi-maryam.html), HSI Lab page (https://hsi.engr.tamu.edu/people/dr-maryam-zahabi/), Google Scholar (https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=LV-JGIcAAAAJ), and ResearchGate. She is listed as Associate Professor, Department of Industrial & Systems Engineering, Texas A&M University. Citation counts for seminal papers sourced from Google Scholar profile, verified June 17, 2026. DOIs verified via Crossref API on June 17, 2026: 10.1177/00187208261430069 (recent_1), 10.1177/00187208251367179 (recent_2), 10.1080/00140139.2025.2606779 (recent_3), 10.1177/0018720815576827 (seminal_1), 10.1007/s10055-020-00434-w (seminal_2). All five DOIs returned matching titles and authors from Crossref. No unverified DOIs.
